data.title: Intel forecasts Q3 2026 EPS of $0.38 on $15.8B-$16.8B revenue while planning 2026 CapEx above $20B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intel (INTC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Q2 was another quarter of solid execution. Revenue, gross margin, earnings per share were above our guidance... Strong demand for our products continue to outpace our growing supply.” (CEO & Director Lip-Bu Tan)

  • “Our recent announcement to deepen our collaboration with Google Cloud will help to accelerate the transformation as we fully embrace an AI-first mentality throughout operations.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • “During Q2, our factories across Intel 7, Intel 3 and Intel 18A exceeded internal volume targets, driven by improving yields, better cycle times and increasing wafer starts... We also began risk production of 18A-P.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • “We remain on track for 14A risk production for our internal products in second half of 2027, and we make the decision in Q2 to fully committed to high-volume ramp in 2028.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • “Second quarter revenue was $16.1 billion... Q2 non-GAAP gross margin was 41.8%... We delivered second quarter non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.42... Q2 operating cash flow was $7 billion... approximately $30 billion in cash and short-term investments.” (Executive VP, CFO David Zinsner)

  • “Due to strong customer demand signals, we're raising our outlook for 2026 and now expect our CapEx to be more than $20 billion.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Outlook

  • “We're guiding Q3 revenue to a range of $15.8 billion to $16.8 billion... At the midpoint of $16.3 billion, we forecast gross margin of 42%, a tax rate of 11% and EPS of $0.38, all on a non-GAAP basis.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • “We continue to tightly manage non-GAAP operating expenses to roughly $16.5 billion for the year.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Q2 vs. Q1 guidance language shifted toward larger investment needs: Q1 said 2026 CapEx would be “flat to last year,” while Q2 said 2026 CapEx is now expected to be “more than $20 billion.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Demand and constraints remained the key framing: “Industry-wide supply constraints across wafers, memory and substrates remain the dominant challenge.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Financial Results

  • “Second quarter revenue was $16.1 billion... Q2 non-GAAP gross margin was 41.8%... non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.42.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Segment details highlighted a sharper sequential step-up: “CCPG revenue was $8.9 billion, up 15% sequentially... DCAI revenue was $6.3 billion, an increase of 24% sequentially and 59% year-over-year... Intel Foundry, revenue of $5.8 billion was up 6% sequentially... External foundry revenue was $293 million.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Foundry profitability remained negative but improved: “Intel foundry operating loss in Q2 was $2.1 billion and $348 million better quarter-over-quarter.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Mix and cost actions were central to margins: “The upside was driven by higher revenue, better yields and higher ASPs due to mix and pricing actions.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Q&A

  • Benjamin Reitzes, Melius Research LLC: asked what the higher 2026 and 2027 CapEx implies for foundry customers and packaging. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “The CapEx is fairly broad-based... include advanced packaging... skewed towards the front end,” adding, “this increased investment is a signal of our confidence in customers across all of our business units.”

  • Reitzes, Melius Research LLC: asked about a competitor’s CPU TAM projection and Intel’s ability to fulfill demand. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner responded, “without putting a number out there... we feel like the growth is going to be significant,” and said Intel sees CPU-to-GPU “almost in parity at this point.”

  • Joseph Moore, Morgan Stanley: asked about server share prospects vs. AMD and ARM. CEO & Director Tan answered, “we have a strong road map... Clearwater Forest, Diamond Rapid and also Coral Rapid with the SMT involved,” and added, “Some areas we are still behind, but we are catching up very fast.”

  • Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein: pressed on surprising client strength and second-half client expectations. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said client upside was “largely ASP,” and guided near-term shape: “client will be flattish... Q4... will be soft,” while adding, “we'll pivot as much of the production as possible over to CPUs and data centers.”

  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: asked if late-Q3 capacity implies a Q4 step-up. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “we only guide 1 quarter out,” but added, “we start to see some of the logjam break towards the end of the third quarter... see upside in the fourth quarter,” while noting, “we will not catch up.”

  • Vivek Arya, BofA Securities: asked when external foundry confidence will be backed by customer announcements and how 2027 CapEx should be framed. CEO & Director Tan tied confidence to milestones: “PDK 0.9 for 14A is on track for October... risk production for 14A will be second half of 2027.” Executive VP, CFO Zinsner added, “We'll increase tooling in '26 by 40% relative to '25,” and said on 2027, “we need another quarter or 2 to really solidify the number.”

  • Arya, BofA Securities: asked about funding the stepped-up investment. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “We have over $30 billion of cash... a $10 billion revolver,” and added, “we have... roughly... $10 billion of... noncore assets that can still be monetized,” while also noting, “we may need to tap the capital markets.”

  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about ASIC growth and margins, and about Intel’s role in memory. Executive VP, CFO Zinsner said, “we're probably running at about a $2 billion run rate... we think... we'll be at a $4 billion run rate.” CEO & Director Tan said on memory, “we hired Seok-Hee Lee... memory... becomes the bottleneck,” adding, “Stay tuned.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on sustainability and transparency, focusing on CapEx magnitude/timing, supply bottlenecks, client durability, and share regain (e.g., “when will that confidence be backed by actual customer announcements?”). (Vivek Arya, BofA Securities)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing constraints and execution (e.g., “we will not catch up”). (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Versus Q1, management language sounded more confident on demand and investment posture (Q2: “raising our outlook for 2026” CapEx; Q1: CapEx “flat”), while analyst skepticism shifted from validating demand to probing how quickly Intel can add capacity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 increased the scale of the investment message: Q1 framed 2026 CapEx as “flat,” while Q2 raised it to “more than $20 billion,” and added that 2027 CapEx is expected “significantly above the 2026 levels.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Q2 provided firmer foundry timeline commitments: Q1 said it expected “earlier design commitments... beginning in the second half of 2026,” while Q2 emphasized 14A schedule milestones and a decision “to fully committed to high-volume ramp in 2028.” (CEO & Director Tan)

  • Analyst focus was consistent on supply, CapEx, and competitive positioning; Q2 added more pressure on client strength surprise and the mechanics of gross margin normalization.

Risks and Concerns

  • “Industry-wide supply constraints across wafers, memory and substrates remain the dominant challenge.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • PC demand risk was reiterated: “we expect PC consumption to be subseasonal in the second half of the year and down low double digits percent for all of 2026, impacted by rising memory prices and constraints.” (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

  • Execution and ramp risk remained embedded in margin commentary, with management highlighting early-life-cycle mix effects and supply-chain chokepoints beyond wafers (substates, memory, “T-glass”). (Executive VP, CFO Zinsner)

Final Takeaway

Intel’s Q2 message centered on demand outstripping supply across CPUs and foundry services, with management tying improved execution on Intel 18A, risk production of 18A-P, and concrete Intel 14A milestones to a materially higher investment plan. Management guided Q3 to $15.8B-$16.8B revenue and $0.38 non-GAAP EPS, while describing supply-chain bottlenecks (wafers, substrates, memory) as the main limiter and positioning higher 2026 CapEx (above $20B) as demand-backed and disciplined, with potential additional funding levers including customer prepays and, if needed, capital markets access.

data.title: Knowles forecasts 10%-12% 2026 revenue growth as defense demand drives multiyear orders into 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Knowles Corporation (KN) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “We had another quarter of strong broad-based organic growth as we continue to build on the momentum we saw in the first quarter,” said Jeffrey Niew (President, CEO & Director), adding, “we are beginning to see positive momentum in some of our new growth platforms and markets that bodes well to drive additional growth in 2027 and beyond.”

  • Niew (CEO) highlighted segment demand and order activity, including: “In the defense market, our RF microwave products continue to support strong growth across many communications applications,” and “we are seeing more defense customers coming to us, wanting to place multiyear orders to secure capacity.”

  • On large orders and visibility, Niew (CEO) said, “early in July, we received a $15 million-plus order for a radar application that is expected to ship over 36 months starting in 2027,” and added, “we intend to continue to call these large multiyear orders as we receive them.”

  • Niew (CEO) emphasized Precision Devices ordering strength: “Overall, book-to-bill in Precision Devices was 1.4… Orders in the PD segment were nearly $140 million in Q2… providing me confidence in continued growth in the future.”

  • John Anderson (Senior VP, CFO & Interim Principal Accounting Officer) summarized profitability and cash generation: “We reported second quarter revenues of $167 million… EPS was $0.33… Cash provided by operating activities was $28 million,” and added, “we repurchased 416,000 shares at a total cost of $15 million.”

Outlook

  • Management raised its full-year growth view, with Niew (CEO) stating, “we now expect our revenue growth in 2026 to be between 10% and 12%, well above the high end of our organic revenue growth target of 4% to 6% that we provided at our Investor Day in May of last year.”

  • Anderson (CFO) provided Q3 guidance: “For the third quarter of 2026, revenues are expected to be between $167 million and $177 million… We are projecting EPS to be within the range of $0.34 to $0.38 per share,” and “we’re projecting cash from operating activities to be within the range of $35 million to $45 million.”

  • On the implied Q4 setup and segment mix, Niew (CEO) told Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: “we’re going to see sequential growth from Q3 to Q4,” while also noting, “we have a little bit of a headwind in our MSA segment in Q4… inventory building in the first half of the year.”

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q2 performance (non-GAAP continuing operations basis): revenue of $167 million, EPS of $0.33, and cash from operating activities of $28 million.

  • Segment results included Medtech & Specialty Audio revenue of $69 million with gross margin of 53.1%, and Precision Devices revenue of $98 million with segment gross margin of 40.1%.

  • Cost and balance-sheet items called out by Anderson (CFO) included R&D expense of $9 million, SG&A expense of $31 million, interest expense of $2 million, capital spending of $7 million, quarter-end cash of $50 million, and $131 million of borrowings outstanding under the revolving credit facility.

Q&A

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked about moving parts behind full-year 10% to 12% growth and Q4 cadence; President Niew: “we’re going to see sequential growth from Q3 to Q4,” and “the PD segment will continue to grow at the rates that are similar to what we said,” while adding he had “not been able to fully digest” new hearing-aid industry data that “could be closer to the 4% range” for the end market.

  • Rolland, Susquehanna Financial Group, LLLP, Research Division: asked whether passive-market tightness is influencing Knowles and whether pricing could be higher in 2H; President Niew: “Demand is definitely stronger in the back half of the year than we would have projected at the beginning of the year,” and “the pricing environment is definitely stronger than it was last year, and we’re probably going to get more pricing this year.”

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked what is driving broad Precision Devices growth beyond the energy shipments and about the cadence for the rest of the year; President Niew: “it’s very broad-based,” cautioned about not adding capacity for “transient orders,” and said, “Bookings are strong again in July.”

  • Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about M&A posture given organic strength; President Niew: “we’re being very selective in M&A,” called the Cornell deal “a home run,” and said, “if we find the right deal, we will move forward on the right deal,” while Anderson (CFO) added, “absent M&A, we’ll continue… buying back shares, and we still have some debt to pay down.”

  • Anthony Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked for defense mix and multiyear visibility; Anderson (CFO) said, “Filter is roughly $80 million, 90% of that is defense,” and Niew (CEO) said customers are signaling “significant increases in volume over the next 24 to 36 months” and cited the “$15 million” radar order starting shipments in 2027.

  • Stoss, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: asked about PD margin trajectory; Anderson (CFO) said, “we should be in the low 40% range in that PD segment in the back half of the year,” and Niew (CEO) added that pricing actions “don’t really hit the P&L until next year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive tone, with questions focused on durability of the raised full-year growth view, PD demand breadth, pricing leverage, defense visibility, and the pace of margin expansion (e.g., Stoss: “more bullish on the military defense… What kind of visibility?”).

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing breadth and durability of demand (e.g., Niew (CEO): “Orders are super high,” and Anderson (CFO): “our… results… combined with the robust backlog… give me confidence”).

  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from expecting growth “above the high end” of targets in Q1 to a tighter, explicit full-year revenue growth range in Q2 (“between 10% and 12%”), while analyst questioning shifted more toward Q4 cadence and pricing strength as demand tightened.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance framing tightened quarter over quarter: in Q1, Niew (CEO) said 2026 revenue growth would be “above the high end” of the 4% to 6% target; in Q2, he provided a specific 2026 range of “between 10% and 12%.”

  • Precision Devices order commentary strengthened: Q1 book-to-bill was “1.19”; Q2 book-to-bill increased to “1.4,” alongside explicit discussion of multiyear defense orders, including a “$15 million-plus” radar order shipping over “36 months starting in 2027.”

  • Q&A emphasis evolved from energy-ramp execution and capacity utilization discussions in Q1 to broader industry tightness, incremental pricing leverage, and more detailed defense mix and visibility in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Demand pacing risk in Medtech & Specialty Audio was acknowledged, with Niew (CEO) pointing to “a little bit of a headwind in our MSA segment in Q4” tied to “inventory building in the first half of the year,” while reiterating “no real problems there, 2% to 4% for the full year.”

  • Capacity and order-quality discipline was a repeated theme, with Niew (CEO) cautioning against expanding for “transient orders” driven by broader industry lead-time dislocations.

  • Execution risk around expanded PD capacity investments was flagged indirectly through planned spend, with Niew (CEO) saying the company is “looking at taking up capacity in a number of areas in the PD segment for 2027,” while keeping CapEx “at that 5% of revenue.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a continuation of “strong broad-based organic growth,” paired with a more explicit full-year growth framework of 10% to 12% for 2026 and Q3 guidance calling for revenue of $167 million to $177 million and EPS of $0.34 to $0.38. In Q&A, leadership tied durability to a strong Precision Devices booking environment, increasing pricing tailwinds, and improving defense visibility—highlighted by multiyear customer behavior and a $15 million-plus radar order beginning shipments in 2027—while acknowledging a Q4 MSA headwind linked to earlier inventory building.

data.title: Old Republic expects ECM to run at a 90%-95% combined ratio, as it flags a bargain purchase gain next quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old Republic International (ORI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "In the quarter, we produced $238 million of consolidated pretax operating income... and our consolidated combined ratio was 95.3%" (President, CEO & Director Craig Smiddy), and "for the first 6 months of the year, growth in book value per share, including dividends, stands at 7.2%."
  • "Specialty Insurance grew net premiums earned by 2.3%... and produced $199 million of pretax operating income" while "Title Insurance... grew premiums and fees by 10%... and produced $56 million of pretax operating income" (President, CEO & Director Smiddy).
  • "We reported net operating income of $186 million for the quarter... $0.76 compared to $0.83" and "net investment income increased just over 6%..." (Senior VP & CFO Francis Sodaro). He also said Specialty’s runoff transactional risk business "had poor claims experience, which led to reserve strengthening of $40 million in the quarter."
  • "We paid nearly $77 million in dividends and repurchased $61 million worth of our shares, leaving us with about $640 million remaining" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).
  • "Title reported premium and fee revenue for the quarter of $773 million" and "our expense ratio improved by 4 percentage points to 92.1% from 96.1%" (President Carolyn Monroe). She added, "implementation began earlier this year and will continue through the end of next year" on the Qualia operating system rollout.

Outlook

  • "As a precursor to next quarter, we expect to report a bargain purchase gain on the ECM acquisition and for ECM's results to be accretive to earnings and book value this year" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).
  • "The ECM acquisition should contribute to top line and bottom line in the second half of the year" (President, CEO & Director Smiddy).
  • "Our year-to-date combined ratio down to 97.4% as we continue to make progress towards driving our combined ratio below 95%" (President Monroe).
  • Compared with Q1, management again emphasized expense pressure from modernization, but in Q2 tied the current quarter’s Specialty expense ratio to ongoing tech investment: "our continued investments... technology modernization, data analytics and AI accounts for most of that difference" (President, CEO & Director Smiddy).

Financial Results

  • "Net operating income of $186 million" and "$0.76" per share were reported for Q2 (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).
  • "Net investment income increased just over 6%" and "the total bond portfolio book yield ended the quarter at 4.8%" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).
  • "We ended the quarter with book value per share of $25.33" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).
  • Specialty quarterly dynamics included "reserve strengthening of $40 million" in runoff transactional risk (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro) and "the loss ratio for the quarter was 65.9%" with "the expense ratio for the quarter... 29.6%" (President, CEO & Director Smiddy).
  • Title quarter details included "premium and fee revenue... $773 million" and "pretax operating income... $56 million, up from $24 million" (President Monroe), with improvement partly linked to "a onetime litigation settlement expense" last year.

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about intensified price competition and pressure in E&S and property; President, CEO & Director Smiddy said cat-exposed property is small for ORI and "in total, property, we were down about 7.5% in rate," adding Old Republic Property "has not begun to write premium" and "there is no incentive whatsoever in the first 3 years to put any premium on the books."
  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked how ROI is measured on tech/AI spend; President, CEO & Director Smiddy said about expense ratio drivers, "about a full percentage point... is being driven by IT systems investments, data and analytic investments and AI investments," and "we don't have a specific number for you, but there's just no choice that we have to modernize our systems."
  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: Asked about Title commercial momentum (including data centers); President Monroe said, "we really expect to see commercial continue as it has already this year" and noted "a real mix" beyond data centers.
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Asked why commercial auto shows reserve releases alongside a higher accident-year pick; President, CEO & Director Smiddy said, "we raised the 2025 accident year loss pick" and for 2026 "we said... let's just be conservative and put up a bit of a higher loss pick," while prior years developed favorably.
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Asked about a pause in repurchases; President, CEO & Director Smiddy said repurchases remain opportunistic and the company is "very mindful of being dilutive to book value per share," while Senior VP & CFO Sodaro added, "we were issuing shares related to the ECM acquisition. So we were staying out of the market while that was taking place."
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Asked how to model ECM; President, CEO & Director Smiddy said ECM has "the exact same combined ratio targets... somewhere between a 90% and a 95%" and that the external quota share was eliminated "effective July 1."
  • Matthew Carletti, Citizens JMP: Asked about Supreme Court freight-broker liability and Great West exposure; President, CEO & Director Smiddy said, "we insure the truckers... not the freight brokers," and added freight brokers may shift toward "higher-quality" carriers.
  • Matthew Carletti, Citizens JMP: Asked about auto warranty premium “noise” and whether it repeats; President, CEO & Director Smiddy responded, "yes. You should expect it to continue" and said the business is scaling through "a couple of large significant partnerships."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on price competition, reserve posture, and repurchase cadence (e.g., "increasing intensity of price competition" and "Anything to read into that?") (Charles Peters, Raymond James; Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler).
  • Management tone was slightly positive but firm on discipline and modernization, using definitive language such as "there's just no choice that we have to modernize our systems" and forward-leaning phrasing like "we feel good about the prospects" (President, CEO & Director Smiddy).
  • Versus Q1, Q2 carried more pointed discussion of adverse reserve action in a runoff line ("reserve strengthening of $40 million") and more emphasis on acquisition-related near-term accounting ("bargain purchase gain") (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 consolidated results were framed by President, CEO & Director Smiddy as "$238 million" pretax operating income and a "95.3%" combined ratio, versus Q1 where he reported "$211.5 million" and a "96.6%" combined ratio.
  • Reserve narrative shifted from Q1’s "favorable development in both Specialty Insurance and Title Insurance" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro, Q1) to Q2 where favorable Title development was "partially offset by unfavorable development from Specialty Insurance" tied to runoff transactional risk (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro, Q2).
  • Capital return cadence slowed quarter-to-quarter: Q1 repurchases were "$161 million" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro, Q1) versus Q2 "$61 million" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro, Q2), with Q2 citing ECM-related share issuance as a factor.
  • Title margin message strengthened: Q1 still described a combined ratio "100%" (President Monroe, Q1), while Q2 highlighted progress and a target direction: "make progress towards driving our combined ratio below 95%" (President Monroe, Q2).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Runoff transactional risk business had poor claims experience" resulting in "reserve strengthening of $40 million" (Senior VP & CFO Sodaro).
  • Specialty profitability and growth trade-offs remained explicit: "while we're seeing some top line pressure in Specialty Insurance, we continue to focus on bottom line combined ratios" (President, CEO & Director Smiddy).
  • Title mix/commission pressure was noted: margin improvement was "slightly offset by higher agent commissions due to a greater weighting of agency business" (President Monroe).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q2 results included weaker Specialty underwriting versus last year due to adverse runoff transactional risk development, while Title’s profit and efficiency improved on higher transaction volumes and cost actions. The company highlighted acquisition-related catalysts, with the CFO expecting a bargain purchase gain next quarter and management positioning ECM as accretive and targeting a 90%-95% combined ratio over time, alongside continued capital returns constrained in Q2 by deal-related share issuance.

data.title: Robert Half outlines Q3 revenue of $1.31B-$1.41B as Protiviti targets $45M annualized savings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Robert Half (RHI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, Global Enterprise revenues were $1.336 billion" and "Net income per share in the second quarter was $0.26," while "Hiring demand continues to improve and market conditions are increasingly more supportive of our business." (Vice Chairman, President & CEO M. Waddell)
  • "Talent Solutions delivered its third consecutive quarter of sequential revenue growth on an adjusted basis" and "permanent placement operations, also posted adjusted year-on-year revenue growth of 2.5%," while management said "second quarter EPS was impacted by cost actions taken by Protiviti." (President & CEO Waddell)
  • "As we discussed in our last call, Protiviti revenue results reflect ongoing shifts in the U.S. financial services regulatory environment" and "cost actions were taking during the quarter, including $7 million in severance costs, which reduced adjusted gross margin by 1.4 percentage points or $0.04 per share." (Executive VP & CFO Michael Buckley)
  • "Technology was our strongest performing practice group within contract Talent Solutions, achieving adjusted year-over-year revenue growth of 2.3%" and client activity increased "particularly in technology modernization, data, cybersecurity and IT infrastructure." (President & CEO Waddell)

Outlook

  • "With that in mind, we offer the following third quarter guidance: revenues, $1.31 billion to $1.41 billion. Income per share $0.43 to $0.53." (CFO Buckley)
  • "Our midpoint revenue guidance for the third quarter reflects year-over-year growth of 3% for Talent Solutions and 6% lower revenues for Protiviti." (CFO Buckley)
  • Compared with last quarter’s framing that Q3 Talent Solutions would return to "1% to 3%" year-over-year adjusted revenue growth and that Protiviti Q3 margins were estimated at "7% to 9%," the current call formalized Q3 enterprise guidance and reiterated that Protiviti headwinds "are expected to continue into Q4." (President & CEO Waddell; CFO Buckley)
  • "Because of the November and December holidays, the fourth quarter has 61.1 billing days" and "fourth quarter operating margins have historically been 0.5 to 1.5 percentage points lower sequentially than third quarter margins." (CFO Buckley)

Financial Results

  • "On an adjusted basis, second quarter Talent Solutions revenues were down 2% year-over-year" and "On an adjusted basis, Global second quarter Protiviti revenues were down 5% versus the year ago period." (CFO Buckley)
  • "Reported operating income for the second quarter was negative $62 million" while "Adjusted operating income was positive $39 million in the quarter or 2.9% of revenues." (CFO Buckley)
  • "For Protiviti, gross margin was 13.5% of Protiviti revenues" and "Adjusted gross margin for Protiviti was 18.5%" as results included "a $101 million gain from investments held in employee deferred compensation trusts" that "has no effect on our reported net income." (CFO Buckley)
  • "Cash flow provided by operations during the quarter was $109 million" and "Return on invested capital for the company was 9% in the second quarter." (President & CEO Waddell)

Q&A

  • Mark Marcon, Baird: asked whether Risk & Compliance deteriorated further and whether tech projects were delayed; President & CEO Waddell replied, "Risk and compliance is a little under 20%, Protiviti's total revenues" and "Protiviti's technology consulting practice group reported the best revenue quarter in its history and clearly was not impacted by project delays."
  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: pressed on international Protiviti weakness and bill-rate deceleration; President & CEO Waddell said, "particularly Germany, they had some large public sector engagements that wound down" and on bill rates, "it was only...30 basis points different than the prior quarter" and "if our bill rate growth is less, so as our pay rate growth."
  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: asked for Protiviti-specific Q4 margin seasonality; President & CEO Waddell said, "we did not break out one from the other" and cited "the impact of regulatory" plus "you'll lose one more billing day this year...about $20 million in revenue."
  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO: asked about wage inflation and returning to peak margins; President & CEO Waddell said, "underlying wage inflation has come down" and "Absolutely not" (on limits to prior peak margins), adding "I actually am bullish that we can get to new peak margins."
  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: asked steady-state staffing growth and whether regulatory pressure is temporary; President & CEO Waddell said "somewhere mid-ish single digits" and on AML demand, "the changes are more about the current administration" and "there's probably pent-up demand being created."
  • Kartik Mehta, Northcoast: asked when Protiviti laps regulatory headwinds and pricing; President & CEO Waddell said, "starting first quarter of 2027, you'll get some relief" and "the pricing environment...remains so today, but not more so."
  • Tobey Sommer, Truist: asked if Q4 seasonality is normal and about public sector; President & CEO Waddell said, "we're returning to normal seasonal patterns" and "we like our opportunities in the public sector...The pipeline is solid."
  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: asked for restructuring walk-through and contractor mix; President & CEO Waddell said, "in Q2, we had $0.04 of severance" and "Q3...all those savings...have been embedded in the guidance," and added, "it's more project-driven than that" on contractor vs. full-time mix.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on Protiviti’s regulatory exposure, international softness, and visibility; questioning included "what caused the weakening" and how "atypical" Q4 margins could be. (William Blair’s Romeo; JPMorgan’s Steinerman)
  • Management was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more confident in Q&A, emphasizing recovery language and margin protection; President & CEO Waddell said, "we feel great about Protiviti's future" and "we feel good given we've already had three straight quarters."
  • Versus last quarter’s more cautious positioning around a "false start or 2" and guidance framed as conservative run-rate carryover, the current call leaned more assertive on cycle positioning, with "Talent Solutions is the bottom end" and repeated comments about returning to "normal seasonal patterns." (President & CEO Waddell)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 shifted from Q1’s emphasis on tax-rate-driven EPS pressure to Protiviti execution, with Q2 described as impacted by "cost actions" including "$7 million in severance costs" and "an annualized cost savings of $45 million" that management said are "fully reflected in our third quarter guidance." (CFO Buckley; President & CEO Waddell)
  • Talent Solutions messaging progressed from "a second consecutive quarter of positive sequential growth" (Q1) to "third consecutive quarter" (Q2), alongside an explicit callout that permanent placement returned to year-over-year growth. (President & CEO Waddell)
  • Analysts’ focus was consistent quarter-to-quarter on Protiviti’s regulatory-driven Risk & Compliance outlook and staffing recovery durability, but Q2 included more probing on international Protiviti declines and bill-rate deceleration. (Baird’s Marcon; William Blair’s Romeo)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macro and geopolitical uncertainty, with President & CEO Waddell noting "inflation remains a key concern" including "escalating tensions in the Middle East."
  • Protiviti risk was tied to regulatory mix-shift dynamics; President & CEO Waddell said the practice saw "a marked decline in new enforcement actions" and work moving to projects "shorter in duration and have different staffing and leverage characteristics."
  • On mitigation, President & CEO Waddell said Protiviti "acted decisively to better align its resource base" and that actions produced "an annualized cost savings of $45 million."

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q2 as a staffing recovery in progress, with Talent Solutions extending sequential momentum and permanent placement returning to year-over-year growth, while Protiviti remains pressured by U.S. financial services regulatory shifts and a changing engagement mix. Leadership emphasized cost actions and embedded savings supporting Q3 guidance, and repeatedly characterized technology demand as resilient across both staffing and Protiviti, while flagging inflation and geopolitical uncertainty as ongoing watch items.

data.title: Old Second projects net interest margin around 5.18% in Q3 and 5.15% in Q4 as buybacks continue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old Second Bancorp (OSBC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "From a GAAP perspective, net income was $28.2 million or $0.54 per diluted share in the second quarter and return on assets was 1.65%." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board James Eccher)
  • "Second quarter earnings were impacted by $9.2 million of net loan charge-offs, which primarily included 2 credits that we discussed at length on last quarter's earnings call." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Our financials reflect an exceptionally strong net interest margin of 5.23% for the second quarter." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Asset quality trends improved during the quarter despite the elevated charge-offs." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Overall, the bank continues to perform at an exceptionally high level. Operating leverage is strong. The margin is stable and fee income businesses are performing well." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Net interest income increased to $83.3 million for the quarter relative to last quarter's $81.1 million and increased by $19 million or almost 30% from the year ago quarter." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Bradley Adams)
  • "The result obviously in a reduction to equity and growth in the treasury stock of $15.4 million." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams)

Outlook

  • "If I had to guess, I would say that we would be at kind of a 5.18% range in the third quarter and maybe 5.15% in the fourth." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams)
  • "Loan growth for 2026, still target low to mid-single digits." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams)
  • "We are cautiously optimistic due to the improvements in credit metrics this quarter." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)

Financial Results

  • "Tangible book value per share increased to $14.77 at the end of the quarter from $14.35 last quarter." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "The tangible equity ratio increased 12 basis points from last quarter from 11.07% to 11.19%." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Provision levels quarter-over- linked-quarter decreased by $2.5 million to $7.5 million." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Non-interest income increased $631,000 or 5% in the quarter compared to the prior linked quarter." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "Total noninterest expense for the second quarter increased $1 million from the prior linked quarter." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)

Q&A

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler & Co.: "what do you think is kind of a better kind of projection in terms of where charge-offs can shake out"; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher: "we haven't seen anything new really pop up in the last couple of quarters that had not been previously identified" and "we're really close to having a very clean quarter on the credit front."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler & Co.: "thoughts on how the margin could trend in the back half of the year"; Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams: "we would be at kind of a 5.18% range in the third quarter and maybe 5.15% in the fourth" and "the biggest delta on margin... is the speed of attrition on effectively wholesale deposits and our success in backfilling."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler & Co.: "what the appetite and kind of prospects are on the acquisition front"; Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams: "Well priced M&A that adds something to our franchise value is something we're always interested in" and "I think it's reasonable to expect that we will fully execute this authorization as well over the next 12 months."
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: "what rate is kind of needed now to generate that core deposit growth"; Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams: "it would be margin accretive to replace it with wholesale funding" and "it's just a question of how much wholesale funding are you willing to stomach."
  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: "On the C&I loan, is that still on balance sheet"; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher: "Yes. No, it's still on balance sheet" and "I expect that credit to be fully resolved within the next quarter."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: "any one-timers in that 5.23%"; Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams: "no, I'm not aware of any one-timers that positively impact the margin."
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: "the Evergreen kind of merger costs as well as cost saves"; Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams: "I believe so, yes."
  • Kenneth Kohut, Raymond James: "at what point do share repurchases not make sense"; Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams: "I'm not sensitive to it" and "buying back fully this authorization would still not result in capital levels going down."
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: "how you think credit plays out"; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher: "I'd like to say we're going to get back into that 35 to 45 basis points" and "we hope to see further reductions in classified and NPAs."
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: "the special mentions were"; President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher: "They were down $12.5 million in the quarter, from about $40 million to $27 million."
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: "your outlook for that contribution margin"; Director, EVP & President, National Specialty Lending Darin Campbell: "I expect that to continue through this year with some reduction coming next year" and "you will see it come down a little bit in '27."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Obviously, the margin is ridiculously good at this point." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams) and "I expect that credit to be fully resolved within the next quarter." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler & Co.: "some nice cleanup" and "what do you think is kind of a better kind of projection"; Brandon Rud, Stephens: "what rate is kind of needed"; Kenneth Kohut, Raymond James: "how sensitive you guys are"—analyst tone leaned neutral to slightly skeptical, centered on charge-offs, margin durability, deposit competition, and buyback discipline.
  • "we are cautiously optimistic" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher) in prepared remarks aligned with "we haven't seen anything new really pop up" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher) in Q&A; compared with Q1’s "obviously, a mixed quarter" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher), management language shifted toward improving credit trajectory.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "Nonperforming loans decreased $19 million" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher) contrasted with Q1: "Nonperforming loans increased $22.7 million" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher), alongside Q2: "Asset quality trends improved" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher) versus Q1: "Asset quality trends softened" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher).
  • "Total loans increased $60.6 million during the second quarter" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher) contrasted with Q1: "experienced a decrease in total loans of $66.9 million" (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher), with management reiterating: "Loan growth for 2026, still target low to mid-single digits" (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams).
  • "From a stock repurchase perspective, we acquired 732,000 shares during the second quarter" (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams) contrasted with Q1: "we acquired 1.2 million shares" (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams), alongside Q2: "the Board of Directors have approved a new plan to repurchase approximately 2.5 million shares through June 30, 2027." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams)

Risks and Concerns

  • "The office portfolio continues to be under pressure broadly with valuations coming in at steep discounts to prior levels and rents declining broadly." (President, CEO & Chairman of the Board Eccher)
  • "the uncertainty with the war in Iran results in some reluctance on borrowers to invest in capital projects." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams)
  • "both loan and deposit market competition is very robust right now." (Executive VP, COO & CFO Adams)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 around a higher net interest margin, improving criticized/classified trends, and continued capital return, while acknowledging elevated—but increasingly concentrated—credit clean-up. Executives emphasized a loan growth target of low to mid-single digits for 2026, guided margin modestly lower to about 5.18% in Q3 and 5.15% in Q4, and signaled sustained buyback intensity under a new authorization through June 30, 2027, while keeping M&A interest focused on “well priced” deals that “add something” to franchise value.

data.title: Popular outlines 8%-9% 2026 net interest income growth while raising ROTCE target to 14%-17%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Popular, Inc. (BPOP) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “Before going into our results, I'd like to comment briefly on this morning's announcement about my retirement at the end of August.” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Javier Ferrer-Fernández)

  • “We reported net income of $278 million and earnings per share of $4.35, an increase of $0.57 per share or 15% from the first quarter.” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández)

  • “Earlier this morning, we announced a planned 20% increase in our quarterly dividend to $0.90 per share and a new $1 billion share repurchase authorization.” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández)

  • “We performed ahead of our expectations across nearly all categories as our teams continue to be focused on executing their business plans in support of our key strategic objectives.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Jorge Garcia)

  • “Given the strength of our results and confidence in our ability to deliver sustainable returns, we are establishing a higher annual ROTCE objective of 14% to 17%.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Credit quality remained stable during the second quarter... reported credit metrics this quarter were primarily influenced by 2 significant commercial developments.” (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer of Corporate Risk Management Group Lidio Soriano)

Outlook

  • “Our loan growth guidance remains consistent from last quarter at the low end of the 3% to 4% range.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Given the favorable funding trends in Puerto Rico and balance sheet growth, we now expect net interest income to increase between 8% and 9% for the year.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Therefore, we expect NIM to remain generally stable for the rest of the year.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “We now expect quarterly noninterest income to be in the range of $165 million to $170 million for the year.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Based on current trends, we expect full year expense growth to remain at approximately 2% to 3% for the year, including profit sharing expenses.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “We now expect the effective tax rate for the year to be between 14% and 15% due to higher projected exempt income.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Given our year-to-date commercial charge-offs and NPL inflows this quarter, we now expect net charge-offs to be in the range of 65 to 80 basis points for the full year.” (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano)

Financial results

  • “Net interest income increased by $23 million to $693 million.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Noninterest income increased by $15 million to $181 million and was above our guidance range.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Operating expenses increased by approximately $17 million to $484 million.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “Tangible book value per share increased by $2.96 to $87.94, while CET1 increased 16 basis points to 16.1%.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “We resolved our largest nonperforming relationship, a $155 million commercial loan... During the quarter, we recognized a $71 million charge-off and transferred the remaining $84 million balance to loans held for sale... The sale was completed on July 2 for $84 million.” (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano)

Q&A

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: “have you changed your underlying expectations for optimal capital levels? And should we think of that $1 billion as a 12-month goal for buybacks?” Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “the authorization does not have a time limit... we would be executing buybacks for the rest of the year in the $300 million to $400 million range... we're still out there considering the additional Tier 1 capital... the market rates are just not in our favor right now.”

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: “What would have to happen... to see loan growth expectations move higher?” Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “we do see fewer of those large ticket loans... resolving the $155 million loan this quarter, that also reduces the net growth of the loan portfolio.”

  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: “Any thought around what loss content could be there” on the two new nonaccrual C&I relationships? Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano: “a lot of the driver for the provision this quarter was those 2 loans actually... We did not specifically talk about the amounts. But... they're adequately reserved.”

  • Brett Rabatin, The Benchmark Company: “talk about... increased competition... in the U.S. and Puerto Rico.” President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández: “competition is still rational... we will defend our turf and price rationally.” Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “in the U.S., we are seeing continued competition, particularly in Florida and New York... tough competition in the online channels where really the yield is your competitive advantage there.”

  • Brett Rabatin, The Benchmark Company: expenses cadence. Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “We do expect expenses to go higher in the second half... our annual salary increases... probably adds $4 million or $5 million a quarter... the guide does include the range of profit sharing.”

  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist: deposits ex-government down. Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “There is seasonality... it's just really the cycle of tax refunds and then people using those tax refunds... where we see the big increase in outflows is in POS, so interchange.”

  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist: consumer credit strength and tax refunds. Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano: “there is seasonality in our consumer performance... the level of delinquencies and charge-offs are below the same period last year.”

  • Timur Braziler, UBS: NIM stability. Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “when we say stable, we do mean stable for the rest of the year... we've increased our target for public funds by 10%... there's a little bit of basis risk there.”

  • Timur Braziler, UBS: “are we nearing that kind of peak-ish level here right now?” President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández: “I don't think we're nowhere near our peak... we're just beginning.”

  • Timur Braziler, UBS: mainland M&A and NOLs. Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “We are not going to drive an acquisition strategy to realize the benefit of that DTA.” President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández: “whole bank M&A is not a priority.”

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: efficiency and expense levers. Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia: “we had about $50 million in savings across the organization... We're not trying to slow down a project to try to meet an expense guidance.”

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: normalized net charge-offs. Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano: “Our mortgage back in the days... had losses of around 1%. Over the last 3 or 4 years have been net recoveries rather than losses.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive, emphasizing capital deployment, profitability durability, and credit specifics, including “optimal capital levels,” “peak-ish level,” and loss-content questions on new nonaccruals.

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A on profitability and strategy, including “we are establishing a higher annual ROTCE objective of 14% to 17%” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia) and “we're just beginning” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández).

  • Versus last quarter, the discussion shifted from sustaining a 14% ROTCE “through the cycle” to a higher 14% to 17% objective, alongside a CEO succession announcement and larger capital return actions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership changed materially: “my retirement at the end of August” and “Jorge has the experience... to lead this organization” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández), versus Q1’s continuation of the existing leadership team.

  • Profitability framing moved from “remain focused on reaching our 14% through the cycle objective” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández, Q1) to “a higher annual ROTCE objective of 14% to 17%” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia, Q2).

  • Capital return messaging intensified from Q1’s expectation to “exhaust” a remaining $126 million authorization in Q2 and “pursue a dividend increase during the year” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia, Q1) to Q2’s “20% increase... to $0.90 per share” and “a new $1 billion share repurchase authorization” (President, CEO, Corporate Secretary & Director Ferrer-Fernández).

  • Credit narrative shifted from a “single borrower in the telecommunication industry” driving specific reserves (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano, Q1) to the resolution of “a $155 million commercial loan” and new nonaccrual inflows of “approximately $129 million” (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano, Q2).

Risks and concerns

  • “2 unrelated commercial and industrial relationships totaling approximately $129 million were placed on nonaccrual status.” (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Soriano)

  • “While higher balances of Puerto Rico public deposits contribute to NII growth, their higher cost is expected to temper some of the benefit to margin.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

  • “We're also seeing tough competition in the online channels where really the yield is your competitive advantage there.” (Executive VP, CFO & Corporate Finance Group Garcia)

Final takeaway

Management emphasized a leadership transition alongside a quarter it described as “very strong,” highlighted by $278 million of net income, a higher profitability target of 14% to 17% ROTCE, and increased capital return through a planned dividend raise to $0.90 per share and a new $1 billion repurchase authorization. Guidance was updated to 8% to 9% net interest income growth and stable NIM, while credit commentary centered on resolving a $155 million nonperforming exposure, two new nonaccrual C&I relationships, and a higher full-year net charge-off expectation of 65 to 80 basis points.

data.title: SS&C expects 2026 adjusted EPS of $6.93-$7.25 as it raises full-year revenue outlook to $6.672B-$6.832B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SS&C Technologies (SSNC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Q2 was record-setting for SS&C" (CEO Bill Stone), adding that the quarter set "quarterly records for second quarter in revenue, adjusted EBITDA, earnings per share and shares repurchased" and that results were supported by "new business wins, strength in multiyear renewals and market and transaction tailwinds" (CEO Stone).
  • "This quarter, we returned $499 million to shareholders, which includes 6.4 million shares repurchased for $435.2 million, the highest quarterly buyback in our history" (CEO Stone), and he said "share repurchases remain our top capital allocation priority" (CEO Stone).
  • "Calastone continues to perform ahead of expectations" (CEO Stone), and he described an expanded digital-investment roadmap: "The new capabilities will enable digital investment transactions to settle using regulated forms of digital cash, including stable coins and tokenized commercial bank deposits" (CEO Stone).
  • "The highly anticipated Medicare GLP-1 Bridge Program launched on July 1" (CEO Stone), and he said, "Since launch, nearly 3 million claims have been processed using SS&C's DomaniRx platform" (CEO Stone).
  • "Our second quarter results came in ahead of expectations with strong sales and renewal performance and continued margin expansion" (President & COO Rahul Kanwar), and he said SS&C is "deploying AI across our products and operations" with a "governance first" approach (President & COO Kanwar).
  • "Our Q2 2026 GAAP results reflect revenues of $1.696 billion, net income of $235 million and diluted earnings per share of $0.97" (CFO Brian Schell), and he added that adjusted results included "revenues of $1.697 billion" and "adjusted diluted EPS of $1.76" (CFO Schell).

Outlook

  • "For the third quarter of '26, we expect revenue to be in the range of $1.657 billion to $1.697 billion" (CFO Brian Schell), with "5% organic revenue growth at the midpoint" and "adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $1.73 to $1.79" (CFO Schell).
  • "For the full year 2026, we increased our expectations to revenue to be in the range of $6.672 billion to $6.832 billion" (CFO Schell) and "adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $6.93 to $7.25" (CFO Schell), while reiterating "EBITDA margin expansion of 50 basis points with the goal of a 40% margin in Q4" (CFO Schell).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, Q2 guidance language added a more specific profitability milestone: "with the goal of a 40% margin in Q4" (CFO Schell), while management again emphasized "a stronger weighting to share repurchases versus debt reduction" (CFO Schell).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted consolidated EBITDA, also a record, was $671 million" (CFO Brian Schell) with "a margin of 39.5%, a 50 basis point expansion" (CFO Schell).
  • "The adjusted revenue increase of $159 million was primarily driven by incremental revenue contributions from organic growth of $118 million, acquisitions of $36 million and a favorable impact from foreign exchange of approximately $5 million" (CFO Schell), and he said "core expenses increased 6.1% or $58 million" (CFO Schell).
  • "Cash flow from operating activities grew 11.1%, driven by growth in earnings" (CFO Schell), and SS&C ended Q2 with "$435 million in cash and cash equivalents and $7.6 billion in gross debt" (CFO Schell).
  • "Resulting net leverage ratio is 2.75x" (CFO Schell), and he said SS&C is "actively exploring refinancing opportunities for our bonds maturing in 2027" (CFO Schell).

Q&A

  • Daniel Perlin, RBC Capital Markets: asked why organic growth ran above guidance and whether license renewals were a factor; CEO Bill Stone: "we did have a couple of big renewals in Q2, and that's why organic revenue was up a couple of hundred extra basis points" and said ASC 606 can create "somewhat of a rush on revenue" depending on timing.
  • Daniel Perlin, RBC Capital Markets: asked how market activity may have influenced results; CEO Stone: said the hedge fund "redemption index was at 1.35%, which is about the lowest we've had" and added that assets under administration in fund administration were up "$581 billion" over the last two years.
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked if AI is driving outsourcing demand; President & COO Rahul Kanwar: customers want to "take advantage of AI" and "Agentic capabilities," and SS&C can "deploy it in a very scalable way" (President & COO Kanwar).
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked about GIDS growth excluding lift-outs and the lift-out pipeline; CEO Stone: said the pipeline is strong and that underlying growth "It's still in excess of 5%" while calling lift-outs "very accretive" but "competitive" (CEO Stone).
  • Kevin McVeigh, UBS: asked what the Medicare claims launch contributed and how it scales; CEO Stone: "We get in excess of double of the $0.20" and said it is "a 6-month program" with continuation "probably 50-50" (CEO Stone).
  • Isabella Camaj, JPMorgan: asked why Q3 organic growth implies a step down and whether upside renewals exist; CEO Stone: "Our pipeline is full of opportunities" and said, "we have opportunities to outperform" but "we're not going to pre-beat the drum" (CEO Stone).
  • Peter Heckmann, D.A. Davidson: asked about Intralinks’ sequential improvement; President & COO Kanwar: said pipeline, bookings, and conversion metrics "are positive" and added, "we are seeing the business strengthening" (President & COO Kanwar).
  • Michael Infante, Morgan Stanley: asked whether AI affects renewals as monetization upside or pricing pushback; CEO Stone: said SS&C’s approach is "showing people that our technology is better" and emphasized guardrails and orchestration to deliver "very sophisticated workflows that are not people intensive" (CEO Stone).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, repeatedly calling out results and then probing sustainability and drivers, including organic-growth "lumpiness" from renewals, AI’s role in outsourcing demand, and durability of Intralinks improvement.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and remained confident in Q&A, using phrases such as "pipeline is full of opportunities" and "opportunities to outperform" while also showing caution on the Medicare program: continuation "probably 50-50" (CEO Bill Stone).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from emphasizing macro headwinds (Q1 referenced "war" and "tariffs") to emphasizing "record-setting" execution, while analysts’ focus shifted toward renewal timing, AI-driven demand, and specific program monetization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 emphasized records and buybacks, with CEO Bill Stone highlighting "the highest quarterly buyback in our history," while Q1 emphasized macro disruption and said, "we are raising 2026 guidance" amid geopolitical and tariff concerns.
  • Q2 discussion elevated tokenization from preparedness (Q1) to roadmap and settlement mechanics, including "regulated forms of digital cash" (CEO Stone), and added operating detail on DomaniRx’s Medicare claims scale.
  • Analysts in Q2 concentrated on drivers of organic outperformance (renewal timing, market/transaction tailwinds) and the monetization posture around AI and Agentic automation; Q1 analysts more frequently pressed on macro sensitivity, segment risks, and capital return cadence.

Risks and Concerns

  • "It depends when these things hit" (CEO Bill Stone) described renewal-driven revenue timing as a source of quarterly variability due to ASC 606.
  • "It's a 6-month program" (CEO Stone) framed the Medicare GLP-1 Bridge Program as time-limited, and he said continuation is "probably 50-50" (CEO Stone).
  • "We are actively exploring refinancing opportunities for our bonds maturing in 2027" (CFO Brian Schell) highlighted upcoming debt maturities and ongoing capital-structure actions.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as "record-setting" and tied outperformance to large renewals, organic growth, and margin expansion while reinforcing a capital-return stance centered on buybacks. Guidance was raised for full-year 2026, with targets that include a Q4 EBITDA margin goal of 40%, alongside continued investment in AI (including Agentic automation) and an expanded tokenization roadmap via Calastone, while flagging renewal timing variability and the uncertain renewal of the 6-month Medicare GLP-1 program.

data.title: Five Star Bancorp outlines Southern California expansion and plans another office later in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Five Star Bancorp (FSBC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Before we discuss the results of the second quarter of 2026, we wanted to note that, as you may be aware, we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock.” (President, CEO & Director James Beckwith)

  • “As the offering has not yet settled, today we will present only financial results for the second quarter of 2026 and will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Q2 2026 was another period of strong performance, reflecting the continued execution of our organic growth strategy and the strength of our relationship-driven model.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Net income increased to $19.4 million compared to $18.6 million in the first quarter of 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “In April, we announced the expansion of our business in Southern California with the addition of 5 seasoned banking professionals to lead our efforts across the Greater Los Angeles area.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “With plans to open an additional Southern California office later this year, we believe we are well positioned to deliver Five Star Bank’s high-touch concierge banking services to the clients and communities of Greater Los Angeles.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “We also continue to execute on our footprint expansion with the opening of our newest full-service branch in Lodi on July 13.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “The Lodi branch further strengthens our food, agribusiness, and diversified industries vertical.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Net interest income increased to $46.1 million in Q2 2026, a 6.04% increase from $43.5 million in Q1 2026.” (Executive VP & CFO Heather Luck) “Net interest margin contracted by 7 basis points quarter-over-quarter to 3.63%.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck)

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks, and no analyst-estimate comparison is included because the call did not disclose guidance figures for EPS or revenues.

  • “Ongoing uncertainty surrounding geopolitical tensions, energy market instability, and uncertainty around the pace and direction of the Federal Reserve monetary policy have contributed to a more volatile interest rate environment.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “We believe we are well positioned to navigate these conditions, as approximately 74% of our loans held for investment are adjustable or floating, providing meaningful flexibility to respond to market shifts.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s earnings call, management’s updated prepared remarks emphasized the equity offering and the lack of Q&A: “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock” and “will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Financial Results

  • “Earnings per share rose to $0.91 per share, up $0.04 from Q1 2026, and up $0.23 from the second quarter of 2025.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Net interest margin of 3.63%, a decrease of 7 basis points from the prior quarter, and average cost of total deposits of 2.16%, an increase of 3 basis points from the prior quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Loans held for investment grew by $306.3 million, or 29% on an annualized basis, reaching $4.5 billion at June 30, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Total deposits grew by $330 million, or 30% on an annualized basis, with non-wholesale deposits up $463.1 million, more than offsetting the $133.1 million reduction in wholesale deposits.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Ratio of non-performing loans increased to 0.30% at June 30, 2026, up from 0.07% at March 31, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “This increase was attributable to one Community Reinvestment Act loan that was placed on non-accrual status during the period.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “The balance of the loan is $11.4 million as of June 30, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “We recorded a $2.3 million provision for credit losses during the quarter, primarily related to loan growth.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Our allowance for credit losses totaled $47.3 million at June 30, 2026, up from $44.4 million at December 31, 2025.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Non-interest expense increased by $2.2 million in Q2 2026 compared to Q1 2026, primarily due to a $1 million loss contingency release on an SBA loan that benefited Q1 and did not reoccur during Q2.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck) “Our efficiency ratio was 40.91% for Q2 2026 compared to 38.57% in Q1 2026.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck)

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript: “we will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Sentiment Analysis

  • With no Q&A, analyst tone was not observable in the current quarter prepared remarks; in the prior quarter, analysts pressed on growth, deposit mix, margins, and expenses, including questions like “Any updated expectations on pace of balance sheet growth or targets for the year?” (Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc.: Question)

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive and confident, emphasizing execution and positioning: “With the first half of 2026 behind us, we are encouraged by the results we have delivered and confident in our position rolling into the second half of the year.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • Versus Q1 2026, management confidence language remained consistent, while Q2 added a more procedural note around the offering and format: “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering” and “will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 highlighted an equity-capital action and removed live Q&A, while Q1 ran a full analyst session: “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock” and “will not be conducting a Q&A session today.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • On balance-sheet mix, Q2 described a further shift toward core funding: “Non-wholesale deposit accounts constituted approximately 94.79% of total deposits at June 30, 2026, up from 91.43% at March 31, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • On credit discussion, Q2 added specific NPL-driver detail not present in Q1 prepared remarks: “This increase was attributable to one Community Reinvestment Act loan that was placed on non-accrual status.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • On growth strategy messaging, Q2 emphasized geographic execution steps already taken (Los Angeles build-out and Lodi opening): “addition of 5 seasoned banking professionals” and “opening of our newest full-service branch in Lodi on July 13.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Ongoing uncertainty surrounding geopolitical tensions, energy market instability, and uncertainty around the pace and direction of the Federal Reserve monetary policy have contributed to a more volatile interest rate environment.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Approximately 74% of our loans held for investment are adjustable or floating.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • “Ratio of non-performing loans increased to 0.30% at June 30, 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “The loan is well collateralized, and a current appraised value provides significant cushion in excess of the carrying amount of the loan.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)

  • Funding-cost pressure was framed through deposit repricing and mix: “average cost of total deposits of 2.16%, an increase of 3 basis points from the prior quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith) “Growth in the average balance of non-interest-bearing deposits by $41.9 million helped partially offset the rise in deposit funding costs.” (Executive VP & CFO Luck)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 2026 around continued organic balance-sheet growth, an underwritten common-stock offering that changed the call format, and ongoing California footprint expansion, while also acknowledging margin pressure from funding costs and a specific nonaccrual migration tied to a single CRA loan; as CEO James Beckwith put it, “we also announced yesterday the pricing of an underwritten public offering of shares of our common stock,” and “with plans to open an additional Southern California office later this year,” the bank said it remains “disciplined in our approach to growth and managing risk.” (President, CEO & Director Beckwith)
